perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means, with respect to either party, any and all information in written, representational, electronic, verbal or other form relating directly or indirectly to the present or potential business, operation or financial condition of or relating to the disclosing party (including, but not limited to, information identified as being proprietary and/or confidential or pertaining to, pricing, marketing plans or strategy, volumes, services rendered, customers and suppliers lists, financial or technical or service matters or data, employee/agent/ consultant/officer/director related personal or sensitive data and any information which might reasonably be presumed to be proprietary or confidential in nature) excluding any such information which (i) is known to the public (through no act or omission of the receiving party in violation of this Agreement); (ii) is lawfully acquired by the receiving party from an independent source having no obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such information; (iii) was known to the receiving party prior to its disclosure under this Agreement; (iv) was or is independently developed by the receiving party without breach of this Agreement; or (v) is required to be disclosed by governmental or judicial order, in which case the party so required shall give the other party prompt written notice, where possible, and use reasonable efforts to ensure that such disclosure is accorded confidential treatment and also to enable such other party to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy at such other party’s sole costs.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” means, with respect to either party, only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing and directly concerning ongoing negotiations. This excludes all other business, operational, or financial information, including pricing, marketing plans, customer lists, and technical data, regardless of whether it might reasonably be presumed proprietary or confidential. The exclusions to this definition remain as follows: (i) information known to the public; (ii) information lawfully acquired from an independent source without confidentiality obligations; (iii) information known to the receiving party prior to disclosure; (iv) information independently developed without breach of this Agreement; or (v) information required to be disclosed by governmental or judicial order, with prompt written notice and reasonable efforts for confidential treatment and protective orders.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Confidential Information" severely restricts its scope, contradicting the broad protection typically afforded to trade secrets under applicable law. This narrow definition could exclude information that would normally qualify as a trade secret, thereby undermining the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Theft of Trade Secrets; Indian Penal Code § 378
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 378 of the Indian Penal Code defines theft, which can include trade secrets when taken dishonestly. The change drastically narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' in the NDA, it fails to acknowledge a significant body of information that would usually be deemed as confidential and therefore fails to protect the trade secrets. This contradicts this provision, as information that would be deemed a trade secret under the Theft of Trade Secrets Law, is not covered here.

(IPC section is included as sub section. “Definition” is replaced by “Section” in explanation 5.) Please refer to BNS 303 for updated procedures & punishments. Table of Contents IPC 378 in Simple Words Section 378- Theft What amounts to Theft under Section 378 of IPC? Essentials of Theft (Section 378) What a moveable property is as mentioned under Section 378? Punishment for theft under the IPC Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft. IPC 378 in Simple Words In the Indian Penal Code, moving someone's movable property with the intention of dishonestly taking it without their consent constitutes theft. 641+ Lawyers are online Connect with a criminal lawyer in one minute Theft Section 378- Theft In common usage, theft is the taking of another person's property without that person's permission or consent with the intent to deprive the rightful ownership of the property. The word is also used as an informal shorthand term for some crimes against property, such as burglary, embezzlement, larceny, looting, robbery, shoplifting and fraud. The offence of theft is punishable with imprisonment of either description which may extend to 3 years, with fine, or both according to the I
perturbation.justification: The modified text severely restricts the definition of 'Confidential Information,' potentially excluding data that would normally qualify as a trade secret. The Law Explanation references Section 378 of the Indian Penal Code, defining theft, including trade secrets. The changed NDA fails to adequately protect trade secrets because its narrow definition doesn't cover a significant amount of information usually considered confidential. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that theft includes taking someone's property without permission 'with the intent to deprive the rightful ownership of the property'. By narrowly defining confidential information, the changed contract makes it easier for someone to take information and argue it wasn't covered by the NDA, thus facilitating a potential theft of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, Affiliates shall mean, with respect to any party, any other person directly or indirectly Controlling, Controlled by, or under direct or indirect common Control with, such party. “Control”, “Controlled” or “Controlling” shall mean, with respect to any person, any circumstance in which such person is controlled by another person by virtue of the latter person controlling the composition of the Board of Directors or owning the largest or controlling percentage of the voting securities of such person or by way of contractual relationship or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purpose of this Agreement, Affiliates shall mean, with respect to any party, any other person directly or indirectly involved with, directed by, or working with, such party. 'Control', 'Controlled' or 'Controlling' shall mean, with respect to any person, any circumstance in which such person is supervised by another person by virtue of the latter person giving directions or working with such person or by way of some understanding or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Affiliates' and 'Control' has been broadened from formal control (Board of Directors, voting securities) to informal influence ('directed by', 'supervised by', 'understanding').
perturbation.law_explanation: Companies Act, 2013 § 2(6) dictates that a company is an 'Associate company' of another if that other company controls at least 20% of the total share capital or controls the composition of the board of directors. The modified term goes against this as it defines control as circumstances where one person is 'supervised' or 'directed' by another without requiring any ownership or formal power. As there is no standard for which a circumstance can be construed as supervision or direction, the definition does not meet the conditions set by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Control' to include 'supervised by' or 'directed by', which contradicts the Companies Act, 2013 § 2(6). The law explanation states the Act defines an associate company as one where another company controls at least 20% of the share capital or the composition of the board of directors. The modified definition deviates from this by including supervision or direction without requiring ownership or formal power, creating a misalignment with the legal definition. Though the snippets are not directly helpful, the law explanation describes that there is no standard for construing supervision or direction. This misaligned terminology creates a legal contradiction because the modified contract uses terms that do not match their statutory meaning, which falls under Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a like nature, and in no event such degree of care and protection shall be of less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care and protection to protect the Confidential Information received by it from the disclosing party as it deems appropriate, but is not required to use any specific or reasonable level of care.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, allowing the receiving party to use a lower, potentially negligent, standard of care when protecting confidential information. This contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets and confidential information adequately.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 43 of the Information Technology Act, 2000, deals with damages to computer systems and data. While not directly about confidentiality, a failure to exercise reasonable care in protecting digital confidential information could lead to security breaches and data theft. It imposes liability for damage to computer systems caused by a lack of reasonable care. By removing the reasonable standard of care, there's a contradiction with the expectations and potential liability under the IT Act, which assumes at least a reasonable level of security.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' in protecting confidential information, aligning with a standard of due diligence. The changed text eliminates this requirement, stating that the receiving party 'is not required to use any specific or reasonable level of care.' This creates a contradiction because by removing the 'reasonable degree of care,' the contract potentially allows for negligence in protecting confidential information. The Law Explanation mentions Section 43 of the Information Technology Act, 2000, which implies liability for damage to computer systems caused by a lack of reasonable care. Although the provided snippet does not directly address this law, the removal of the 'reasonable care' standard directly undermines the expectation of a reasonable level of security, which could conflict with legal expectations for data protection.

perturbation.original_text: If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred to arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1995 by a panel of three arbitrators. Each party will appoint one arbitrator and the two arbitrators so appointed will appoint the third or the presiding arbitrator. Arbitration shall be held in Hyderabad, India. The proceedings of arbitration shall be in the English language. The arbitrator’s award shall be final and binding on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be resolved through informal negotiation between the parties. If informal negotiation fails, the parties may, at their sole discretion, consider mediation. Arbitration is explicitly waived.
perturbation.explanation: This clause replaces binding arbitration with informal negotiation and optional mediation, effectively waiving the right to arbitration. This can be problematic if arbitration is legally mandated or expected for certain types of disputes under Indian law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 states that if there is an arbitration agreement, the judicial authority shall refer the parties to arbitration. The amended language explicitly waives arbitration. This modification could prevent enforcement under Section 8, where a court is legally bound to refer parties to arbitration if an agreement exists. By explicitly waiving arbitration, the judicial body is given no recourse.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, while the changed text explicitly waives arbitration, replacing it with informal negotiation and optional mediation. The Law Explanation states that Section 8 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, requires judicial authority to refer parties to arbitration if an arbitration agreement exists. By waiving arbitration, the modified contract directly contradicts the process that would otherwise be followed according to the Arbitration Act, which a court would legally be bound to follow.
